feed.subtitle: Software Engineer. Mental Health Worker. Building toward Computational Psychiatry.

entry.title: What the APA Leadership Summit Taught Me

link.title: What the APA Leadership Summit Taught Me

entry.content:
I recently attended the 2026 American Psychiatric Association Leadership Summit in Montgomery, Alabama, from March 5 to March 7. This was my first time attending since being accepted into their fellowship program. What started as a conference trip turned into one of the most clarifying experiences of my life.

I applied to this fellowship a day before the deadline. I did not expect to get in. I had no experience in psychology. I was not even a pre-med student at the time. I stumbled on the application by accident while going through a very tough period last year. I was looking into mental health resources, which led me to the APA website to learn what psychiatry actually is and what psychiatrists do. That is where I found the application.

The program has been incredibly helpful in my preparation for medical school. We have a speaker series featuring everyone from psychiatrists to lawyers, study resources including a funded 12-month Kaplan MCAT preparation course, a current medical student mentor, and a practicing psychiatrist mentor. For this trip, the APA generously covered all of our expenses, including flights, hotel, food, transportation, and lunch during the conference days.

By the time I got on the plane to Montgomery, I was not starting from zero. I had already been working as a mental health worker in an acute psychiatric hospital for several months. I had spent hundreds of hours on the floor doing 15-minute rounds, one-to-one observations, admissions, and transports. I had watched patients cycle through medications that did not work. I had seen intake processes built around liability instead of care. I had ridden in the back of an ambulance and seen how an emergency room uses sensors, imaging, and blood work to find a clot that no human eye could detect, and then walked back into a psych unit where we still documented safety rounds on paper. That gap between what medicine can do and what psychiatry actually does had already become the central question of my life.

That question is what led me to start building. I had been developing two projects under Stratification Labs. Psykick is a platform built to relieve the administrative burden that eats psychiatrists alive. The average psychiatrist spends over ten hours a week fighting insurance companies and filling out forms, more than any other specialty. Psykick automates prior authorizations, generates clinical documentation through an AI scribe, defends claims against bogus denials, handles electronic prescribing, and gives providers a dashboard to see exactly where their time goes. The goal is simple: stop making psychiatrists do paperwork and let them treat patients. Psychify is the clinical research side. It uses foundation models to stratify patient biotypes and predict treatment response at day zero, before the months of trial and error that currently define psychiatric prescribing. If Psykick removes the administrative friction, Psychify goes after the diagnostic uncertainty.

Coming into the summit, I knew the problems. What I did not have was confirmation from inside the medical establishment that I was heading in the right direction. I was not sure how traditional doctors would receive someone approaching psychiatry from an engineering background. I was about to find out.

My trip started with a flight from Los Angeles to Charlotte, then a connection on the smallest plane I have ever been on for the final leg to Montgomery. I arrived around midnight, and finding an Uber at the airport at that hour was its own challenge. During the day, downtown Montgomery was strikingly quiet. I barely saw anyone walking around. Locals told me people come out more at night, but it had a completely different energy than any other American city I have visited. It felt like a place built more for reflection than for rush. I would soon understand why.

The theme of the summit was changing landscapes and informed mental health policy. We heard from clinicians, healthcare researchers, and lawyers with health policy degrees across two days of talks, capped by three book signings. What struck me most was how heavily the speakers focused on wealth. There is an undeniable economic dimension to mental health and well-being, and hearing experts lay that out so clearly was fascinating, especially because I had just finished an introductory class on the U.S. healthcare sector. Much of what the speakers discussed matched what I had learned in class. I remember using the mental health parity law as an example of how the government tries to improve healthcare access through policy. Hearing that same concept discussed at the national level by the people actually shaping these policies made the stakes feel completely different.

This conference validated a lot of what I have written about in the past. My aspiration to become a psychiatrist grew significantly during this trip. It confirmed my belief that many of the problems we face in the mental health space are rooted in inadequate social, political, and economic policies, not just clinical shortcomings.

The summit was held at the Elevation Convening Center, a venue built by the Equal Justice Initiative as a companion to the Legacy Sites. It was not a coincidence that we were there. The building itself has a library, a theater, and learning spaces designed to extend the experience of the nearby museum and memorial. So when we stepped outside the conference room to tour The Legacy Sites, the transition felt natural. We observed a moment of silence for Bloody Sunday. The summit’s final day, March 7, fell on the 61st anniversary of the 1965 attack on voting rights marchers at the Edmund Pettus Bridge in Selma, just 50 miles west of where we were gathered. Commemorations were happening across the state that same weekend.

But The Legacy Museum made the strongest and most emotional impression on me. Growing up in Africa, I understood the history of European colonization and the devastation it caused across the continent. That was the lens I carried with me. My grievance had always been with the Europeans who carved up Africa, extracted its resources, and left behind borders drawn for their convenience. What I had not fully confronted until walking through that museum was the full machinery of what happened on this side of the Atlantic. It was not that the history was new to me. It was that I had never been forced to sit inside it. Seeing how people were priced alongside animals, described in language designed to increase their value to slave masters. Seeing advertisements for public lynchings where thousands would gather to watch. Learning about the repossession of enslaved people as collateral for loans, the physical maiming, and how all of this was perfectly legal. Even after abolition, the exploitation continued. Families were separated by pure greed, the demand for free labor to produce cotton, sugar, and tobacco, and to build public infrastructure.

Seeing that level of historical trauma and economic exploitation firsthand connected directly to the summit lectures on the social determinants of health. The brutal economic practices of the past laid the groundwork for the wealth gaps and mental health disparities that persist in these communities today. That connection was not abstract anymore. It was on the walls in front of me.

This experience also pushed me to engage in a racial conversation for the first time in the ten years since I moved to this country. I typically stay out of racially charged discussions. Because I was born in Africa and only came to the U.S. a decade ago, I have always felt my experiences were different, even though I am deeply sympathetic. My understanding of racial injustice was shaped by what Europeans did to Africa. The American story, the domestic slave trade, the lynchings, the systems built to maintain control after abolition, all of that was something I knew about but had never truly sat with. Before we entered the museum, a Black American attendee asked me if I had experienced racism. I told him I had not, or maybe I just did not know how to spot it. He said that was interesting because another African student in his class had said the exact same thing. I do not have a neat conclusion to that exchange. But standing in that museum, I felt the weight of a history that connects to mine in ways I am still learning to understand and talk about.

Another major highlight of the summit was the people. I interacted with many medical students, residents, and fellows who were incredibly generous with their time. They walked me through what medical school is actually like, shared their different pathways to getting there, and even passed along free study guides for the MCAT. I have been to tech hackathons and conferences where everyone is fired up about what they are building. This summit had that exact same energy, but for medicine. It was the highest concentration of doctors I have ever been around in my life. Being immersed in that environment raised my aspirations higher than ever. For the first time, the goal did not feel distant. It felt like the next step.

I also gained real clarity on where my engineering background fits. During the summit, I learned the specific difference between computational psychiatry and digital psychiatry. Computational psychiatry is more closely associated with neuroscience and brain research. Digital psychiatry focuses on building clinical applications for use in everyday psychiatric practice. I even met a resident who works in a digital psychiatry clinic, where they collaborate directly with software engineers to clinically label AI inputs and outputs. That was a pivotal moment. Not because it told me something I did not already suspect, but because it gave me the exact language for what I had been doing. I had spent months on the floor watching the gap between what psychiatry measures and what it should measure. I had already started building tools to close that gap. Hearing a resident describe a clinic that does this work daily made it real in a way my own research had not.

Working as a mental health worker was the best decision I made. It gave me the real mental models and lived experiences I need to build tools that actually work in clinical settings. The summit confirmed that.

One of the most valuable conversations I had was about the route to medical school itself. I had been open to attending an osteopathic school. I liked their holistic approach to medicine, though I originally thought that philosophy might be more relevant to neurosurgery than psychiatry. The residents clarified that the type of medical degree, whether MD or DO, does not really matter for matching into psychiatry. But the degree type does matter if I want to pursue a dual MD/PhD. Most DO schools do not offer a combined degree, especially not a PhD in Health Policy. And here is the part that stopped me in my tracks: the residents told me I could potentially go to medical school for free through the MD/PhD route.

That changed my entire calculus. I am now strongly considering a PhD in Health Policy instead of Engineering. I already have the engineering fundamentals, and digital psychiatry is not as engineering-intensive as computational psychiatry. A health policy foundation would let me address the systemic problems I keep running into, the ones that no amount of code can fix on its own. The residents also gave me tactical advice I would not have found on my own, like understanding the real difference between schools with strict pass/fail grading versus those that grade pass/fail with honors. These are the kinds of details that shape an application strategy, and I would not have known to ask about them without these conversations.

We also talked about life outside the hospital. I met two residents who are married, and they explained something I had never considered: the unspoken timeline of personal life in medicine. They told me the sweet spot for getting married is the fourth year of medical school, right before residency begins. The best time to have children, they said, is during residency, because that is when you actually get protected parental leave. What fascinated me was not the specifics of the timeline, but what it revealed about the profession. Even the most personal decisions in a doctor’s life are shaped by institutional structures and policies. It was another version of the same lesson the entire summit kept teaching me. Policy is not just political. It is personal.

For me, I am not pressed to get married right now, and I am not sure I want kids. But understanding how these systems work gave me a much clearer picture of what the next decade of my life could look like, regardless of the personal choices I make.

On the day of my departure, I got a firsthand lesson in exactly why policy matters. My original flight was scheduled to leave around 1:00 PM, but the airline canceled it, citing severe weather in connecting cities like Dallas. When the agent who booked my flight through the APA told me there was nothing that could be done to get me home that day, I started looking into it myself. Flights were still departing from Dallas that afternoon. When I checked the actual weather conditions there, they did not look severe at all.

At the airport, I asked the airline customer service agent if they would cover my hotel cost since the cancellation was on their end. He said no. I pushed back and referenced the Biden administration’s automatic refund rule, which requires airlines to compensate passengers when flights are canceled or significantly changed. I also pointed out that flights were still leaving from Dallas, which raised the question of whether this was truly a weather cancellation or an operational decision the airline was attributing to weather to avoid its obligations. As soon as I started citing specific policy, his tone shifted. He told me he was going to make sure I flew out that night. I got on a standby list and boarded a 5:56 PM flight home. When we got to Dallas, the skies were mostly calm with only minor turbulence in the air.

What stayed with me was not just the outcome but the dynamic. The moment I demonstrated that I knew my rights as a passenger, the conversation changed completely. The cancellation that was supposedly impossible to resolve got resolved in a few hours. That interaction crystallized the entire summit for me. Policy does not just exist on paper. It works when people know about it and are willing to use it. But it also fails when people do not know it exists, which is exactly the problem with so many of the mental health policies we discussed at the conference.

This trip completely clarified my next steps. Before attending, I was not sure how my tech background would be received by traditional doctors. Instead of skepticism, I found support. People encouraged me to keep building and told me the medical field desperately needs these skills right now. The problems I had been writing about for months, the diagnostic uncertainty, the administrative waste, the policies that sound good on paper but lack enforcement, those same problems were being discussed at the national level by the people with the power to change them.

Moving forward, I plan to focus on policy research right alongside technology development. The truth is, a tool like Psykick would not even be needed if medical practice was not buried under administrative burdens created by poor policies. Those policies distort everything, right down to the behavior of consumers and insurance companies. On the clinical side, Psychify’s mission to end trial-and-error psychiatry through biotype stratification felt more urgent after hearing speaker after speaker describe the same gaps I had seen on the floor. The technology and the policy have to move together.

I left Alabama knowing exactly what I want to do, knowing what I need to build, and knowing that there is a community of people ready to support me in doing it.
entry.summary: I recently attended the 2026 American Psychiatric Association Leadership Summit in Montgomery, Alabama, from March 5 to March 7. This was my first time attending since being accepted into their fellowship program. What started as a conference trip turned into one of the most clarifying experiences of my life.

entry.title: The Policy Floor
entry.content:
I just finished an introductory course on the U.S. healthcare sector. For months, I had been writing about what I observed as a mental health worker, the administrative chaos, the paper rounds, the insurance-driven discharge timelines, the gap between what patients need and what the system provides. I understood these problems from the floor. Now I was learning the policy architecture that created them.

What surprised me was not how broken the system is. I already knew that. What surprised me was how precisely the incentives are designed to produce exactly the outcomes I see every night.

The foundation of U.S. healthcare billing is a model called Fee-for-Service. Under FFS, providers are paid for each service performed. Every visit, every procedure, every test generates a charge. The flaw is structural: FFS rewards the quantity of services rather than the quality of care. Studies confirm the consequence. Adults in the U.S. receive the generally accepted standard of care only about 55 percent of the time.

This incentive structure explains something I noticed early in my time on the floor but did not have the language for. Psychiatrists see patients for minutes. Not because they do not care, but because the payment model punishes longer visits. If a psychiatrist tries to spend more time with a patient, the practice loses money. The fee per visit is too low to support it. The result is that the most important conversations, the ones that might actually change a diagnosis or a treatment plan, get compressed into slots designed for throughput. Patients wait days to see a psychiatrist and then get a few minutes. I wrote about this frustration months ago. Now I understand the billing code that causes it.

FFS also has no payment for many of the things that matter most in psychiatric care. There are traditionally no fees for phone calls with patients, for emails, for the hours nurses spend coordinating care and prepping discharge paperwork. Because providers are not paid for this work, it gets squeezed into the margins. I watch our nurses buried at the computer every shift, managing compliance documentation, intake prep, discharge paperwork, flagging what doctors need to sign. In my opinion, much of this workload is optimized for compliance and insurance reimbursement rather than patient care, though I stand to be corrected. What I am not wrong about is that it leaves almost no time for the human interaction that patients are desperate for.

The billing system runs on codes. ICD codes classify the diagnosis. CPT codes describe the procedure. These codes determine how much facilities get paid. For inpatient services, hospitals receive a fixed amount per Diagnostic-Related Group. For outpatient services, the payment is based on Ambulatory Payment Classification. The entire system depends on translating a patient’s suffering into a billing category.

This coded system creates its own battles. The most visible one on the psychiatric floor is prior authorization. Prior authorization is the insurer’s decision that a service or medication is medically necessary before they will cover it. According to the AMA’s most recent physician survey, 84 percent of physicians report that the number of prior authorizations required has increased over the last five years, for both prescription medications and medical services. I have seen what this looks like in practice. The patient I wrote about in an earlier essay, the one who eventually transferred from the ICU to the DDMI unit and improved, waited nearly a month for the regional center to clear the transfer. The unit that would help was in the same building. The paperwork was the barrier.

Prior authorization is supposed to control costs. In practice, it delays care, burns clinical hours on administrative appeals, and gives insurers a mechanism to deny coverage for treatments that providers have already determined are necessary. This is the exact problem Psykick is built to solve. Automating prior auth requests, tracking approvals, and generating appeals when claims are denied is not a convenience feature. It is a clinical intervention. Every hour a nurse spends arguing with an insurance company is an hour not spent with a patient.

Here is where California law becomes important. California’s Mental Health Parity Act, significantly strengthened by Senate Bill 855 in 2020, requires all state-regulated health plans to provide full coverage for the treatment of all mental health conditions and substance use disorders listed in the DSM or the ICD. Before 2021, the law only covered nine specific severe mental illnesses, including schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, major depression, and obsessive-compulsive disorder. After SB 855, the mandate expanded to cover the full range of diagnoses.

When I look at our patient population, most of them have conditions that fall squarely under this mandate. Depression. Psychosis. Bipolar disorder. Substance use disorders. The law says their treatment must be covered on parity with medical and surgical care. That means equal copays, equal access, no extra prior authorization hurdles that would not exist for a physical health condition.

But parity on paper is not parity in practice. Insurers are required to comply, but enforcement has been slow and uneven. Health plans have a long history of finding ways to limit mental health coverage even when the law says they cannot. They impose utilization review standards that are stricter for behavioral health than for medical care. They maintain provider networks that look adequate on paper but where a significant percentage of listed providers are unavailable when patients actually try to schedule appointments. The law says equal treatment. The floor says otherwise.

This enforcement gap connects directly to a broader pattern in U.S. healthcare policy. The government passes laws that sound good but builds weak mechanisms to enforce them. Hospital Price Transparency is another example. CMS requires hospitals to publish their prices in machine-readable files so consumers can compare costs. The mandate is clear. But compliance has been inconsistent, and CMS has acknowledged problems with the accuracy and completeness of the data hospitals report. We have a transparency law that does not yet produce transparency.

On the other side of the ledger, insurers engage in practices like downcoding, where they reduce the payment level of a claim by reclassifying the CPT code. The AMA has publicly opposed these practices, arguing that reducing payment based solely on the diagnosis code is inappropriate because the final diagnosis alone does not determine the complexity of the encounter. The provider does the work. The insurer retroactively decides it was worth less. The provider then has to spend time appealing for correct payment, time that could have been spent with patients.

All of this sits underneath the most recent policy earthquake: the One Big Beautiful Bill Act, signed into law on July 4, 2025. The OBBBA made sweeping changes to Medicaid and the ACA marketplace. It imposed work requirements for many adults in Medicaid expansion states. If you do not meet 80 hours a month of work or community service, you can lose your Medicaid and become ineligible for ACA premium tax credits. The Congressional Budget Office projected these changes could leave 16 million more people uninsured by 2034.

The bill also requires states to redetermine Medicaid eligibility every six months instead of annually, tightens eligibility for refugees and asylum seekers, shortens retroactive coverage periods, and imposes new cost-sharing requirements on the expansion population. On the ACA side, the enhanced premium tax credits that had improved affordability are scheduled to expire, and new verification requirements effectively end automatic re-enrollment for subsidized plans.

I do not know how many of my patients are on Medicaid. I am a mental health worker, not a billing administrator. But I know the population I serve. Many of them are homeless. Many of them cycle in and out of the hospital. Many of them have no family support, no stable housing, no job. These are the people Medicaid was designed to cover. If the OBBBA’s changes remove even a fraction of them from coverage, the consequences will not be abstract. They will show up on my floor. They will show up as patients who cannot access follow-up care after discharge, who cannot get their medications, who return to the hospital because the system that was supposed to catch them on the outside no longer exists.

The long-term policy goal is to move away from FFS entirely toward Value-Based Care, where providers are rewarded for outcomes rather than volume. Models like Accountable Care Organizations, bundled payments, and capitation are designed to align financial incentives with actual patient health. Under capitation, for example, a provider receives a set amount per patient and has the flexibility to spend it on things FFS does not reimburse, like care coordination, patient education, and non-medical supports like transportation.

This shift is essential. But it is slow and it is uneven. When Value-Based Care succeeds in reducing expensive services like hospitalizations, hospitals receive less revenue. The payment models have to account for this, and the spending targets they set are often based on historical data that cannot predict new diseases, new drugs, or workforce shortages. The transition is happening, but it is happening while the old system is still running, and the patients on my floor are living inside the friction between the two.

I started this essay by saying that what surprised me in the healthcare course was not how broken the system is, but how precisely the incentives produce the brokenness I see. Fee-for-Service creates short visits and administrative overload. Prior authorization creates delays and denials. Parity laws exist but lack enforcement. Transparency mandates exist but lack compliance. And now the OBBBA is pulling coverage away from the people who need it most.

These are not separate problems. They are one system producing predictable outcomes. The floor is where those outcomes land. The patients do not know about CPT codes or DRGs or the OBBBA’s Medicaid redetermination schedule. They know they cannot see a psychiatrist for more than a few minutes. They know they are waiting weeks for a transfer that should take days. They know that when they leave, there may be nothing on the other side.

Policy is not abstract. It is the reason the nurse is at the computer instead of at the bedside. It is the reason the patient waits. It is the architecture underneath everything I have written about for the past year.
entry.summary: I just finished an introductory course on the U.S. healthcare sector. For months, I had been writing about what I observed as a mental health worker, the administrative chaos, the paper rounds, the insurance-driven discharge timelines, the gap between what patients need and what the system provides. I understood these problems from the floor. Now I was learning the policy architecture that created them.

entry.title: The Machine Doesn’t Know
entry.content:
In January, a patient I knew died in their bed with a CPAP mask on their face.

I met this patient three times across three different units over the course of about a month. The first time was in mid-December. They were in restraints for being disruptive. I negotiated with the nurse to get them released, provided they stayed calm. They did. They paced the hallway and talked to me. They were convinced they had seen me before at another hospital. I do not think they had, but the certainty in their voice made me wonder.

The second time was the week of December 25. The hospital floated me to another unit, and I was assigned as the patient’s CPAP watcher for the night. They barely slept. Maybe thirty minutes the entire shift. The rest of the time, we talked. They told me they did not like using the machine. It was uncomfortable. Hard to move around with. You have to adjust the mask all night. Most patients feel the same way about CPAPs. They tolerate them, but barely.

The third time was in January, on yet another unit. The patient was angry. They had just been told their stay was being extended by 14 more days. I told them to talk to the psychiatrist, since the psychiatrist is the only one who can authorize a discharge. That was the last conversation we had. When my shift ended, the patient waved to me as I walked out.

A few days later, they died in their bed, and the machine kept running after they stopped breathing.

The doctors knew the patient had sleep apnea. The CPAP was at the bedside. A staff member was assigned to watch them. Everything was in place. But after the death, the hospital told us something we had not been told before: the patient had a prior code blue on record. I was never given that information when I was assigned as their CPAP watcher. I sat with this patient for an entire night shift, responsible for monitoring a machine I barely understood, attached to a patient whose cardiac history I did not know existed.

A CPAP machine blows air. That is what it does. In a dark room at night, the sound of the motor is constant. If the patient is breathing, the machine sounds one way. If the patient stops breathing, the machine sounds exactly the same. It does not alarm. It does not alert anyone. It just keeps pushing air into a body that is no longer alive.

As mental health workers, we are not trained on CPAP devices. We know how to plug them in, add water, and turn them on. The screens often go dark after a period of inactivity, and in a dim room, it is impossible to tell whether the machine is functioning correctly. I have seen staff press a button just to wake up the screen, only to accidentally shut the power off because the controls were confusing. We are responsible for monitoring patients on these machines, but we do not understand the machines themselves.

I was not in the unit when it happened. But I was in the building when the code blue was called. I was close to the action when the EMTs came. I did not see the patient. But I felt the shift in the air. The floor got quiet in a way it never gets quiet.

For about a week after, I was in strong disbelief. Not the kind where you question whether it happened. The kind where your mind keeps circling back to it and cannot land anywhere. I have experience with death. I lost my father on a Tuesday, and he had called me on a Sunday. The last time I heard his voice was two days before I lost him. This patient waved goodbye to me at the end of a shift, and a few days later was gone. I do not think I processed the patient’s death separately from my father’s. They layered on top of each other. The pattern was the same. A final, ordinary moment that you do not know is final until it is too late.

I talked to my therapist about it. I talked to my friend who works in social work. I thought about resigning. My original plan had been to leave after six months so I could free up time for studying, but for that week, the reason shifted. It was not about the schedule anymore. It was about whether I could carry this.

I stayed. And something changed in how I work.

Before this patient died, I used to feel guilty about going back to the nurses with observations. They are buried in administrative tasks at the computer, prepping discharge paperwork, intake documentation, flagging what the doctors need to sign, managing compliance requirements and insurance billing. In my opinion, much of this workload is maximized for compliance and payout rather than for patient care, though I stand to be corrected. Every time I walked up to report something a patient said or something I noticed on rounds, I felt like I was taking away from their time. After the death, I stopped feeling guilty. Now I think of that patient every time I do my rounds. If I see something, I report it. I do not care if the nurse is busy. The alternative is worse.

The hospital changed a lot of things after it happened. They fired the three staff members who signed the patient’s rounds from 11:30 PM onward that night. They changed the identification color for CPAP patients to yellow so they are immediately visible. They started requiring licensed staff to do one-to-one monitoring for every CPAP patient, where before an unlicensed mental health worker could be assigned. The house supervisor now rounds on CPAP patients directly. The shift supervisor does additional rounds every two hours on top of the assigned licensed staff.

They gave us handouts on sleep apnea and CPAP operation. They posted signs at the bedside showing how to properly fit the mask. We signed new documentation acknowledging we had been informed. They bought flashlights and pulse oximeters so staff could check on sleeping patients without relying on the sound of the machine. They purchased pillows designed for CPAP use. We now have to document in the electronic health record every time a patient takes the mask off and every time it goes back on.

Then the inspectors came. CMS and the state health department reviewed the case. The hospital prepped us for questions. One finding stood out. CMS noted that the patient’s blood pressure had appeared irregular, but there was no record of whether the mental health worker who took the reading had ever told a nurse. So now, every time we report a vital sign or an observation to an RN, we have to document which nurse we told, by name, in the system.

They also stopped hiring unlicensed mental health workers for new positions and posted registered nurse roles instead. They brought back licensed psychiatric technicians and licensed vocational nurses, roles they had eliminated years earlier because they wanted RNs to sign certain paperwork for insurance purposes. The death forced them to reconsider that calculation.

All of these changes happened after the patient died. Every single one of them could have existed before.

This is what I mean when I write about reactive systems. The protocols that are now standard on my floor, the pulse oximeters, the licensed one-to-ones, the documentation requirements, none of them required new technology. None of them required new legislation. They required a death. The system knew the risks. CPAP machines have always worked this way. Sleep apnea has always been dangerous. The gap between what we knew and what we did was not a knowledge gap. It was a policy gap. And a patient filled it with their life.

There is also an insurance dimension to this that I cannot ignore. CPAP machines are personal medical devices. Insurance companies do not easily replace them. Hospitals do not stock them. Patients are expected to bring their own. If a patient arrives without one, or if theirs is broken, the options are limited. We are a psychiatric hospital, not a respiratory clinic. But sleep apnea does not care what kind of hospital you are in. The separation between psychiatric care and medical care, the same separation I wrote about after my ambulance ride, is not just an intellectual problem. It is a gap that patients fall through. This patient fell through it.

I went online after the death looking for solutions. I found companies building at-home sleep tests that attach to your chest, measure breathing patterns, and send data to an app. They give you a score on how well you slept. But we do not need a sleep score. We need a machine that knows when the patient is dead.

This is the next project for Stratification Labs. I want to build an AI-powered portable CPAP with better safety features for institutional use. It needs a biological alarm that detects when the chest stops rising or the pulse stops and immediately sounds a distinct emergency alert, not just the steady hum of the motor. It needs to be safe for a psychiatric ward, with a hose connection redesigned so patients can move freely without creating a ligature risk. And it needs to be smarter than the machines we currently use. Right now, a CPAP runs the same whether the patient is alive or dead. That has to change.

I am not building this because I think technology solves everything. I have written enough essays by now to make clear that I do not believe that. I am building it because a patient I cared about died, and the machine at their bedside did not know.
entry.summary: In January, a patient I knew died in their bed with a CPAP mask on their face.

entry.title: The Stratification Mandate
entry.content:
If you are reading this, you probably found your way here through one of my posts, a product announcement, or someone who told you about Psykick. However you arrived, I am glad you are here.

This page will tell you what Stratification Labs is, what we believe, what we build, and what we expect from anyone who wants to work with us.

The Problem

Psychiatry is broken in three specific ways.

The first is diagnostic. Psychiatry is one of the few areas of medicine that lacks objective biomarkers for diagnosis and treatment. An ER doctor can order an ultrasound, a CT scan, or a blood panel and know within hours what is wrong with a patient. A psychiatrist cannot. Diagnosis depends on symptoms, self-reporting, and clinical observation. Symptoms overlap. Self-reporting is unreliable. The result is a system built on educated guessing, where half of adults with depression do not improve on their first medication and treatment becomes a months-long cycle of trial and error. If you want to understand this gap viscerally, go shadow an ER doctor for a shift and then shadow a psychiatrist in an acute hospital. The difference will stay with you.

The second is administrative. Psychiatrists spend over ten hours a week on paperwork, more than any other medical specialty. Prior authorizations, insurance appeals, clinical documentation, claims disputes. These burdens are manufactured by the way insurance companies reimburse behavioral health treatments. They eat the hours that should be spent with patients.

The third is political. There are policies on the books that should fix this. The Mental Health Parity and Addiction Equity Act requires insurers to cover behavioral health the same way they cover medical care. But the law lacks real enforcement mechanisms. So the administrative burden persists, insurance companies continue to deny claims at disproportionate rates, and psychiatrists continue to drown in paperwork despite a federal law that says they should not have to.

These problems are known. Organizations like the National Institute of Mental Health, the American Psychiatric Association, and policymakers at every level of government have tried to address them. We are still not where we should be. I do not claim to have all the answers, and I do not think we will change this field overnight. But I believe we can go further than most because we sit at an intersection that almost nobody occupies: computation, policy, and direct clinical experience.

What We Believe

At Stratification Labs, we believe the core failure of modern psychiatry is that it treats a wildly diverse patient population as though their problems all have the same origin. They do not.

Some patients are on our floors because they are homeless, hungry, or grieving. Their distress is real but it is not a brain disease. It is a response to social collapse. Some patients are there because of substance use that mimics or masks a psychiatric condition. And some patients have genuine neurobiological disorders that require medical intervention.

These are three fundamentally different problems. Treating them with the same diagnostic framework and the same tools is why the system fails. You cannot medicate someone out of poverty. You cannot talk someone out of a dopamine dysfunction. And you cannot restrain someone out of a withdrawal syndrome.

We believe the path forward is stratification. Social stratification. Chemical abuse stratification. Biological stratification. Sort the problems before you try to solve them. Build tools precise enough to tell the difference. And design systems that route each patient to the intervention that actually matches their condition.

What We Build

Stratification Labs is the company. Psykick and Psychify are the products.

Psykick attacks the administrative burden. It automates prior authorizations, generates clinical documentation through an AI scribe, defends claims against insurance denials, handles electronic prescribing, and gives providers a time analytics dashboard to see exactly where their hours go. The goal is to give psychiatrists back the time the system has stolen from patient care. Most AI scribes just listen. Psykick acts. It unbundles the EMR to handle reception, prescriptions, and audits so clinicians can stop doing paperwork and start treating patients.

Psychify attacks the diagnostic uncertainty. It uses foundation models to stratify patient biotypes and predict treatment response at day zero, before the months of guessing that currently define psychiatric prescribing. If Psykick removes the administrative friction, Psychify goes after the clinical guesswork.

Long term, we want to build medical psychiatric technologies, hardware and software, the way the ER uses sensors and imaging to aid diagnosis. An ER doctor will not order a CT scan for a headache unless the symptoms are severe enough to warrant it. Now imagine trying to use a CT scan to diagnose a headache caused by stress, lack of sleep, dehydration, or skipping meals. That is the precision gap we are working to close. We want to build the instruments that can tell the difference.

Who I Am

I studied computer science and cybersecurity in college. I work as a software engineer. I also work as a mental health worker in an acute psychiatric hospital, where I have spent hundreds of hours on the floor doing 15-minute rounds, one-to-one observations, admissions, and transports. I have floated across every unit type: adolescents, adults, geriatrics, and patients with dual diagnoses of mental illness and developmental disabilities. I have ridden in the back of an ambulance. I have stood outside seclusion rooms during code greys. I have read the DSM-5 and the ICD-11 from cover to cover.

I am currently enrolled in a psychiatric technician program and earning a certificate in health information systems and data analytics. My goal is to go to medical school and become a psychiatrist. I want to practice Consultation-Liaison Psychiatry and pursue a PhD in Health Policy. I am interested in computational psychiatry, but I also want to sit across from patients and talk to them.

I arrived at this field because my own life fell apart last summer and the calm I felt in the middle of it sent me looking for answers that psychiatry does not yet have. That search led me to the floor, and the floor led me to everything I am building now. If you want the full story, read my essays. They are the foundation of everything on this page.

What We Expect

If you want to work at Stratification Labs, there is a process. It is not conventional. It is designed to make sure you understand the problem before you try to build solutions for it.

Step 1: Read. Go through the materials listed at the bottom of this page. Then write a two-page essay on what went through your mind while reading them. Not a summary. A reaction. What surprised you. What frustrated you. What you disagree with. What you want to know more about.

Step 2: Work the floor. Apply as a per diem mental health worker or unlicensed psychiatric technician at a partner psychiatric hospital. Work for one week. Then write about your experience. What did you see? What did you not expect? What would you change?

This requirement is not optional. I spent hundreds of hours on the floor before I built anything, and it changed what I built and how I built it. You will not understand the problem from a whiteboard. You have to stand in the hallway at 3 AM when a patient is in crisis and feel the weight of a system that is supposed to help them and is failing.

We want people with backgrounds in computer science, artificial intelligence, statistics, machine learning, or physics. We also want people with clinical experience: psychiatrists, nurses, physician assistants. If you come from the clinical side, we will teach you the tech. If you come from the tech side, the floor will teach you the medicine. Either way, you will leave with an understanding of how to apply computational skills to mental health that goes far beyond biology and chemistry.

The requirement that everyone work on the floor is the foundation of how we think. We call it limit thinking. Instead of building from assumptions, you build from the boundaries of what is actually possible given the constraints of the real system. You start with the patient. You start with the floor. You start with what is broken. And then you build from there.

I recommend reading the Asimov Press article on limit thinking linked below. It captures the approach better than I can summarize here.

The Stakes

The psychiatric services industry is worth nearly $300 billion. Almost a billion people globally carry a psychiatric diagnosis. In the U.S. alone, the number is 80 million. The NIMH spends over $45 billion on research annually and fights every year for budget increases.

The toll of psychiatric illness is measured in death and disability. It would be good to reduce that suffering. It is also acceptable to hope we build a sustainable business while doing it. Profit is not our only motivation, but we are not pretending we do not want Stratification Labs to survive and grow. The work does not matter if it cannot sustain itself.

If this resonates with you, start with the readings below.

Further Readings

  • Limit Thinking (Asimov Press)
  • Mental Illness Statistics (NIMH)
  • Mental Health Topics (NIMH)
  • DSM-5 Contents (APA)
  • DSM-5-TR Update, September 2025 (APA)
  • NIMH Statistics
  • Translational Digital and Computational Psychiatry Program (NIMH)
  • Computational Psychiatry (Nature)
  • Mental Illness (Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy)
  • Thomas Fuchs on Philosophy in Psychiatry
  • Transcultural Psychiatry (SlideShare)
  • Psychiatry (Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy)
  • Economic Costs of Mental Illness (Yale)
  • Mental Health Parity Law Review (UIC)
  • MHPAEA Enforcement (DOL)
  • MHPAEA Final Rule Statement (CMS)
  • Psychiatric News: Parity Coverage
  • Psychiatric News: Policy Update
  • Introduction to Sociology (OpenStax)
entry.summary: If you are reading this, you probably found your way here through one of my posts, a product announcement, or someone who told you about Psykick. However you arrived, I am glad you are here.

entry.title: My First Time In An Ambulance

link.title: My First Time In An Ambulance

entry.content:
I almost called out of work today. I was exhausted, worn down by the friction of juggling two jobs and two classes for my DIY pre-med. But I went in, and I am glad I did, because tonight completed my full experience as a mental health worker. I performed the final duty of the role: transport.

Acute psychiatric hospitals do not have emergency medical services. We are a closed loop designed for the mind, not the body. When a patient’s physiology fails, we ship them out to a partner hospital. My shift started at 11:00 PM. By 11:15 PM, I was in the back of an ambulance.

The process began with the internist. Unlike the psychiatrists who diagnose based on speech and behavior, the internist looks at the blood. A patient had a swollen left leg. The blood work and vital signs raised a flag, so the decision was made to transfer them to the ER. The ambulance crew arrived, took vitals again, loaded the patient, and I sat in the back for the ride. The transition was sharp. We moved from the static, timeless air of the locked unit to the kinetic, high-stakes environment of emergency medicine.

The difference in intake was immediate. At our hospital, intake is an administrative drill of liability forms and property searches. The psychiatrist is often just a name assigned later. Here, the expert was waiting for us. The triage nurse demanded allergies, medications, and hold status, screening the patient for self-harm before they were even out of the ambulance. The doctor met the patient at the door. He checked the heart rate and the legs before the patient was transferred to the bed. There was no waiting for an assignment. The decision-maker was the first line of defense.

Then came the technology. In our psych unit, the only monitoring tools are human eyes and paper charts. In the ER, the room became a hub of sensors. EKG technicians hooking up leads to read the electrical rhythm of the heart. An X-ray technician imaging the chest. A phlebotomist drawing blood. An ultrasound technician scanning the leg. Then a CT scan. The patient was dehydrated and could not provide a urine sample, so they hung IV fluids immediately. It was a symphony of objective measurement. We were not guessing what was wrong. We were looking inside the machine.

As someone who comes from tech, I recognized this setup immediately. In infrastructure engineering, we use dashboards like Grafana or Prometheus to visualize the health of systems, applications, and services in real time. The ER is the biological equivalent. It is a high-fidelity observability stack. We were not interpreting symptoms. We were reading the logs.

The diagnosis revealed the gap between signs and pathology. Everyone, including the nurses back at the psych hospital, had suspected a Deep Vein Thrombosis because the patient’s left calf was swollen, reddish, and warm. That was the sign. But the ultrasound told a different story. The actual blood clot was not in the swollen calf where we were all looking. It was high in the thigh, in a spot that looked perfectly normal from the outside. The chest X-ray and CT scan found something worse. A Pulmonary Embolism. The patient had a life-threatening clot in their chest that was completely invisible to the naked eye.

No amount of observation or 15-minute rounds would have found it. We needed the sensors.

That line kept running through my head on the ride back to the psych unit. On our floor, we observe patients around the clock. We watch their behavior, we chart their moods, we note what they eat and how they sleep. But we have no sensors. We have no way to see inside the brain the way the ER sees inside the chest. If a clot can hide in a thigh that looks normal, what is hiding in a brain that presents as “stable”?

But I also observed something else that night. The Entropy Trap exists in medicine too, just in a different form. The ER doctor, armed with millions of dollars of diagnostic technology, spent about forty seconds with the patient at the door. Later, he spent another thirty seconds to confirm the diagnosis and tell the nurses to bring meds and move the patient to the ICU. This was not because he did not care. It was because the system’s efficiency metrics determine funding and workflow. The sensors provided the data, so the conversation was deemed unnecessary.

Efficiency had become its own form of entropy. The more optimized the system, the less human the interaction. The ER had the data to save the patient’s life, but in the process, the patient became a set of readings rather than a person. This is the same problem from the other direction. The psych unit has the human contact but lacks the sensors. The ER has the sensors but loses the human.

This is exactly the gap that Psykick is meant to address. By handling the administrative load, the documentation, the prior authorizations, the insurance fights, we can give clinicians back the time that the system has stolen from the patient relationship. The technology should serve the conversation, not replace it.

This experience also clarified my long-term direction. Standard psychiatry often ignores the body, treating the mind in a vacuum. Standard medicine often ignores the mind, treating the organ in a vacuum. I want to operate at the interface. Consultation-Liaison Psychiatry is the clinical discipline that bridges this gap. The C-L psychiatrist works across departments, called in when a medical patient’s behavior changes or when a psychiatric patient’s body fails. You are forced to stratify constantly. Is the agitation caused by hypoxia, withdrawal, or fear? It is the role of the system administrator for the hospital’s behavioral health.

That is the domain where my computational research belongs. C-L psychiatry integrates labs, imaging, and psychiatric history into a single clinical picture. It provides the high-fidelity signals that algorithms require. I do not see it only as a medical specialty. I see it as the necessary hardware access to build the next generation of psychiatric software.

We need the objective precision of the ultrasound combined with the depth of the therapist. We need to see the clot in the brain, but we also need to hear the person in the bed.
entry.summary: I almost called out of work today. I was exhausted, worn down by the friction of juggling two jobs and two classes for my DIY pre-med. But I went in, and I am glad I did, because tonight completed my full experience as a mental health worker. I performed the final duty of the role: transport.

entry.title: The Ontological Error
entry.content:
The sound of a magnetic lock engaging on a psychiatric unit is final. It is the sharp, mechanical punctuation mark that separates a citizen with rights from a patient under the state’s control. In my 792 hours on the acute floor, I have heard that sound hundreds of times. Standing on the inside, observing the intake process, you realize that this click does not just represent a change in location. It represents a metaphysical shift. The person behind the door has been deemed to have lost their agency.

California Senate Bill 331 proposes to change the rules for that lock. It would define a “mental health disorder” in the Lanterman-Petris-Short Act as any condition listed in the current edition of the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders. To a politician, this looks like a housekeeping measure. To a philosopher, it is a category error. It confuses the map for the territory, and in doing so, it threatens to turn the definition of human liberty over to a private committee that never intended to write a constitution.

The DSM is a masterpiece of descriptive utility. It allows a doctor in Tokyo to communicate with a doctor in Toronto about “Schizophrenia” using a shared vocabulary. But the DSM is nominalist, not realist. It does not describe natural kinds that exist in nature like gold or tuberculosis. It describes practical kinds, clusters of symptoms that tend to appear together. The DSM is theory-neutral. It does not care why you are depressed or look for the biological mechanism. It asks if you have five of these nine symptoms.

SB 331 takes this descriptive tool and treats it as a legal threshold. If a person fits a cluster in the book, they have met the standard for state intervention. But the DSM lists 265 conditions, including Caffeine Withdrawal, Restless Leg Syndrome, and Adjustment Disorder. By tethering the legal definition of mental disorder to this ever-expanding catalog, the state is arguing that descriptions of distress are legally equivalent to states of incapacity. A checklist of behaviors is not the same thing as the loss of the self.

This is where my time on the floor collides with the philosophy of Thomas Fuchs. Fuchs argues that true mental illness is not a software glitch or a wrong thought. It is a fundamental disruption of what he calls “Being-in-the-world,” a breakdown of the lived structure of time, space, and the body. I have seen this breakdown. A patient stands in the middle of the hallway, weeping not because they are sad but because they have lost the ability to synthesize time. The future has collapsed into the present. They are not acting irrationally. They are inhabiting a different phenomenological reality where their agency is structurally impossible.

That is the threshold for removing someone’s liberty.

But I also see patients who are poor, addicted, or eccentric. They might meet the DSM criteria for Substance Use Disorder or Antisocial Personality Disorder, but they have not lost their Being-in-the-world. They are making rational, autonomous choices within a harsh environment. SB 331 ignores this distinction. By focusing on the checklist rather than the phenomenology, it creates a dragnet that allows the state to detain the struggling subject with the same authority it uses for the dissolved subject.

We rely on the DSM because psychiatry is still a science of observation, not measurement. In other fields of medicine, we have objective limits. We do not detain someone for looking diabetic. We detain them because their blood glucose is 40 mg/dL and they are in a coma. The biomarker proves the biological mechanism has failed. In psychiatry, we do not have those biomarkers. We cannot measure the prediction error in a psychotic brain or the reward function in an addicted brain. We cannot mathematically prove that a person’s internal control system is broken. Because we lack these objective tools, we rely on subjective observation. And because subjective observation is flawed, the legal standard for removing someone’s freedom must remain extraordinarily high.

If anyone should know this, it is the field’s own leadership. In 2013, Dr. Thomas Insel, then director of the National Institute of Mental Health, declared that the NIMH would pivot away from using the DSM for research. His reasoning was precise: the DSM has reliability, meaning doctors agree on the label, but lacks validity, meaning the label does not map to a biological reality. Insel put it simply. Biology never read that book.

Dr. Nassir Ghaemi puts a number on this failure. When the DSM-III was published in 1980, it introduced 292 diagnoses. Only 14 of them had valid scientific evidence. The other 278 were invented by committee consensus to unify the profession. That means the document was 95.2 percent unvalidated at its inception. We are not talking about a rough draft that was later refined through decades of research. Many of those original categories remain, largely unchanged, in the current edition. By codifying these committee compromises into law, SB 331 treats a trade union’s negotiated treaty as though it were biological fact.

Ghaemi, a recent president of the Massachusetts Psychiatric Society, has also pointed out that the DSM is the economic engine of the American Psychiatric Association. Royalties from its sales fund a significant portion of the APA’s operating budget. By mandating the DSM as the legal standard for civil commitment, the state is not just adopting a flawed diagnostic tool. It is subsidizing a private organization’s revenue stream and giving that organization indirect power over the legal boundaries of human freedom.

Proponents of SB 331, including the California State Association of Psychiatrists, argue that we should trust clinicians. They say that excluding diagnoses from the law freezes medical practice, and that no doctor would detain someone for Caffeine Withdrawal unless they were truly gravely disabled. But this argument assumes the system operates in good faith. It does not always.

In September 2024, the New York Times published an investigation into one of the nation’s largest psychiatric hospital chains. The investigation described a systematic business model in which patients were lured in with free assessments and then held to maximize insurance payouts. Staff were pressured to exaggerate symptoms to justify continued detention. Quiet patients were documented as “withdrawn.” Polite patients who asked to leave were labeled “combative.” The goal was not clinical stabilization. It was to extend the stay until the insurance authorization ran out. One executive was quoted as saying that if there were insurance days left, that patient was going to be held.

This is the danger of SB 331 in practice. By defining mental disorder as anything in the DSM, we hand these corporations a massive expansion of their catchment area. If the law allows a facility to detain a citizen for Adjustment Disorder, we are effectively removing the legal guardrails that prevent false imprisonment. When the legal standard is subjective, the tie always goes to the house. And the house always wins.

Science writer John Horgan has called this broader pattern “Neurocentrism,” the mistaken belief that we can explain all human behavior by looking at neurons. Despite decades of hype, neuroscience has not produced a single unifying theory of the mind or a single reliable biomarker for mental illness. Yet SB 331 asks us to codify this uncertainty into law, treating the soft descriptions of the DSM with the same legal weight as the hard laws of physics. We are confusing the biological hardware with the social software, assuming that every problem of living is a problem of the brain.

This confusion has consequences measured in lives. A July 2024 study by the Federal Reserve Bank of New York analyzed the causal effects of involuntary hospitalization on the exact population SB 331 aims to capture: marginal patients. The findings were devastating. For these patients, involuntary commitment nearly doubled the probability of dying by suicide or overdose in the months following release. The study found that the disruption to the patient’s social stability, their housing, their employment, their relationships, outweighed any clinical benefit of the hospitalization itself. By widening the net to catch these marginal cases, we are not saving them. We are statistically increasing their mortality. We are destroying their social stability to treat a biological condition we have not even validated.

The definition of who gets to be free is the most sacred duty of a democracy. It should be defined by the Constitution and the people, not by a private manual published in Virginia and revised by committees subject to academic trends, pharmaceutical lobbying, and cultural shifts. If California ties its civil liberty laws to the current edition of the DSM, it is outsourcing its sovereignty. It is saying that if a private committee decides next year that Prolonged Grief is a disorder, a citizen in California can lose their freedom for grieving too long. We are confusing a billing manual for a Bill of Rights.

On the floor, amidst the noise and the paper charts, the difference between struggle and sickness is something you can feel. The staff knows it. The patients know it. We do not need a wider net. We need a more precise instrument. Until we have the computational tools to objectively prove that a mind has lost its capacity for agency, and until we can stratify the biological break from the social struggle, we must not allow a book of symptoms to become a weapon of the state.

We must protect the distinction between the map and the territory, because once the lock clicks shut, the territory is the only thing that matters.
entry.summary: The sound of a magnetic lock engaging on a psychiatric unit is final. It is the sharp, mechanical punctuation mark that separates a citizen with rights from a patient under the state’s control. In my 792 hours on the acute floor, I have heard that sound hundreds of times. Standing on the inside, observing the intake process, you realize that this click does not just represent a change in location. It represents a metaphysical shift. The person behind the door has been deemed to have lost their agency.

entry.title: The Entropy Trap
entry.content:
We often describe the psychiatric unit as a place of containment or safety, calling it a “therapeutic milieu.” But if you analyze the acute unit as a system, interrogating the inputs and outputs, it functions as something else entirely. It is a stochastic noise generator designed to maximize uncertainty.

For the past few months, I have observed the floor not just as a worker but as a systems engineer, watching patients spiral not because of their pathology but because of their environment. What I see looks like a fundamental collision between the neurobiology of the brain and the architecture of the facility.

To understand why the current model fails, you have to look at what the brain actually is. Modern computational neuroscience, led by thinkers like Karl Friston, suggests the brain is not a “thinking machine” so much as a predictive processing engine. Its primary biological imperative is to model the world and minimize surprise, or in systems terms, to minimize entropy. The brain wants its internal model to match external reality. Sanity is effectively just predictability.

Psychosis, by this definition, is a prediction error. The brain cannot reconcile the sensory input, so it floods the system with dopamine to flag the discrepancy. A psychotic brain is a brain experiencing maximum internal entropy, a gyroscope that has lost its center and is desperately searching for a pattern to latch onto.

The tragedy is what we do with this brain. We take this organ, already drowning in internal chaos, and we place it in the most unpredictable environment in modern society.

Consider the data inputs of the locked unit. The acoustics are hard and reflective, amplifying random screaming that occurs without warning. Roommates are swapped based on bed availability rather than compatibility. The lighting is artificial and static, detaching the patient from the circadian rhythm of the sun. Staff members interrupt sleep every 15 minutes for safety checks, fragmenting the only biological homeostatic reset mechanism the brain has.

We are trying to stabilize a prediction error by feeding it more noise.

This creates a recursive system error I call the Entropy Trap. The patient enters with high internal entropy, mania or psychosis. The unit responds with high external entropy, noise and threat. The patient’s brain tries to predict the environment but fails, so it increases the gain on the error signal. It releases more dopamine to find the signal in the noise, which only deepens the psychosis and hyper-vigilance. Every slam of a door registers as a threat. The patient screams or hits. We respond with chemical restraints.

We label this “non-compliance” or “agitation.” But mechanically, it is a feedback loop. The architecture of the facility is actively fueling the pathophysiology of the disease. We are trying to put out a fire by suffocating it while the building itself pumps in oxygen.

I have likened this reaction to a dog attack. When a dog attacks, you do not think. You act. Your amygdala overrides your inhibition. In the acute unit, the dopamine flood creates what I call a “False Dog.” The chaos of the floor convinces the brain it is under attack. The patient is not choosing violence. They are reacting to a biological imperative driven by the sensory data we are feeding them.

I experienced a minor version of this system failure myself just days ago. I had gone two nights without proper sleep because I was working obsessively on a new project. My brain was running high on salience from excitement and low on energy. While sleeping on a friend’s couch, a new environment that keeps the brain partially alert, I began to dream. In the dream, I was explaining my project. But because my system was overheated, the biological firewall that usually paralyzes our muscles during sleep failed. The signal leaked through. I turned around physically and began speaking to my friend in reality. I was convinced I was still in the dream. For a few seconds, I was technically psychotic. My internal model overrode the external reality. I was acting on data that was not there.

If a healthy brain can glitch like this after 48 hours of sleep deprivation and high focus, imagine the state of a patient who has been homeless, sleepless, and terrified for a month. We are all running on the same hardware. Their operating conditions are just infinitely worse.

This mechanism is not theoretical. In May 2024, the New York Times profiled Matthew Tuleja, a former Division I football player with OCD. He was not psychotic in the traditional sense. He was overwhelmed. When he was cornered in a small exam room by nine staff members, his brain did not analyze the legal implications. It recognized a physical threat. His athletic training kicked in, the hot system overriding the cool system, and he attempted to run through the line like a fullback finding a hole. The system read this biological survival response as “combativeness.” They pinned him, cuffed him, and injected him with antipsychotics. He was not fighting because he was dangerous. He was fighting because the environment had spiked the entropy to a level where fight or flight was the only available logic. We took a brain in distress, surrounded it with threat, and then punished it for trying to survive.

I watched this mechanism play out in real time on my shift tonight. A patient who had been readmitted was assigned to a room with someone who was trying to sleep. The readmitted patient, known for chronic insomnia, was awake and talking, keeping the other person up. When I asked the roommate if they were okay or needed medication, the readmitted patient immediately accused me of blaming them. They interpreted my neutral check-in as a hostile implication that they were a burden. Their brain, flooded with the stress of readmission and the social friction of the shared room, had predicted a threat where none existed. They were in the middle of a False Dog attack.

But thirty minutes later, something remarkable happened. The patient came back and apologized. This is the crucial data point. It proves that the aggression was not a character flaw but a transient hardware crash. Once the adrenaline flushed out of their system and the prefrontal cortex came back online, their theory of mind returned. They could see me again not as a threat, but as a person. The tragedy is that the facility created the conflict by placing an insomniac with a sleeper, increasing the social entropy of the room. Then we blamed the patient for reacting to the friction we engineered.

This brings us to Trauma-Informed Care. Clinicians often speak of this as creating safety and trust for patients whose nervous systems have been rewired by abuse or neglect. But we treat it as a soft skill, something staff do with their voices or their manners. We ignore the fact that the building itself is often traumagenic.

A traumatized brain is biologically hypersensitive to unpredictability. It interprets sudden noise or social chaos as a threat. By placing a trauma survivor in a high-entropy unit, we surround them with the very triggers their brain is desperate to avoid. Entropy reduction is the architectural prerequisite for Trauma-Informed Care. You cannot ask staff to create a sense of safety in a building designed to generate chaos.

The failure of modern psychiatry is that we try to treat the biological layer while ignoring the environmental layer. We treat the software bug but run it on corrupted hardware. We need to move toward what Dr. Ghaemi calls “Method-Based Psychiatry.” If the problem is biological, like dopamine dysregulation, we use biological methods. If the problem is environmental, like entropy, we use environmental methods. Trying to cure an entropy problem with a biological pill is not just ineffective. It is a category error.

A true therapeutic facility needs to be hyper-predictable. This does not mean total silence, which creates a safety risk because staff need to hear if a patient is in distress. We need to hear the signal while eliminating the noise. Right now, our hard walls and floors reflect every sound, amplifying a dropped book into a gunshot. A therapeutic design would use acoustic dampening materials that absorb echoes and soften impacts without blocking the cries for help. It would prioritize private spaces to remove the social threat variable of a volatile roommate. It would use circadian lighting to restore the temporal variable.

We cannot talk about stabilizing a patient until we stabilize the signal they are receiving. Until then, we are not healing them. We are just muting the noise we created.
entry.summary: We often describe the psychiatric unit as a place of containment or safety, calling it a “therapeutic milieu.” But if you analyze the acute unit as a system, interrogating the inputs and outputs, it functions as something else entirely. It is a stochastic noise generator designed to maximize uncertainty.

entry.title: Why Psychiatry Needs Stratification
entry.content:
I had a moment a few weeks ago where I thought psychiatry was fake. Not the suffering. The suffering is real. But the system we have built to address it. After 62 days of working as a mental health worker, I was watching patients get diagnosed, watching medications fail, watching the constant cycle of switching drugs or increasing dosages, and none of it seemed credible. I started trying to understand why insurance denies so many behavioral health claims, why hospital policies feel so rigid, why so much of what I was seeing on the floor did not match what the textbooks said it should look like.

I was becoming heartbroken. I had come to this field full of optimism that I could use my computational skills to help. Now I was questioning the entire foundation.

So I went to the source. I read the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders from cover to cover. Then I read the ICD-11. Then I started looking into the history of psychiatry and how the DSM was actually developed. What I found did not reassure me. The history of psychiatry is barbaric. The development of the DSM itself is problematic and not particularly scientific. It helps explain why there have not been many breakthroughs in the field for a long time. The system is built on a shaky foundation.

When I compared the DSM labels to the actual behaviors of the patients I was observing on the floor, they did not line up in most cases. The patient’s attitude, their culture, their entire life context seemed to tell a different story than the diagnosis in their chart. I kept seeing a gap between the label and the person.

This is where my background matters, and it is not the background you might expect. In high school, I was an art and humanities student. I fought my placement in the science track because I wanted to study law and political economy. I loved debating. I was analytical in a way that leaned toward systems and arguments, not equations. I looked into the Philosophy, Politics, and Economy program at Oxford. I never got to study that. I came to the U.S. and studied computer science instead. But that early wiring never left. It is why I think I see the floor differently than most people in my role.

When I started working the night shift, I found myself constantly asking questions and looking for answers while I observed patients. I started scribbling notes on puzzle scratch papers. Then I bought a small notebook. I realized I was doing philosophy and psychology simultaneously, trying to understand not just what was happening to these patients but why the system responded to them the way it did.

I know I am not a doctor or a nurse practitioner. But I spend more time with the patients than either of them, thanks to the 15-minute rounds and the one-to-ones. My job is literally to observe and record behavior. From that position, I see a disconnect that I believe the people making diagnoses from brief encounters cannot always see.

Mental illness is highly cultural. I see people admitted for behaviors that, back home, would just get them called “stubborn” and never placed in psychiatric care. The baseline for what counts as mental illness shifts depending on where you are from. More than that, I hold a strong belief that mental illness is in large part driven by the absence of strong social support and the state of our socioeconomic system. When I look at our patients, I do not see a uniform population with brain disorders. I see people whose problems have wildly different origins being funneled into the same diagnostic framework and treated with the same tools.

This is why medical psychiatry on its own feels so limited. The only things that feel genuinely medical on our unit are the medications, the vitals, and the blood and urine draws, which mostly exist to rule out pregnancy or other physical conditions during admission. Everything else is observation and conversation. Yet we treat the entire population as though the answer is pharmaceutical.

This led me to a hypothesis that I kept refining in my notebook. We need stratification. Not one kind. Three kinds.

Social stratification of mental illness. Many of our patients are homeless, hungry, grieving, or trapped in poverty. Their distress is real, but it is not a brain disease. It is a response to impossible conditions. These patients need housing, case management, job support, and community. Medicating them without addressing the social collapse is like putting a cast on a leg that is not broken.

Chemical abuse stratification of mental illness. Substance use disorders overlap with and mimic almost every psychiatric diagnosis. A patient in methamphetamine psychosis looks a lot like a patient with schizophrenia. But the causes are different, the trajectories are different, and the treatments should be different. Lumping them together muddies the data and leads to interventions that miss the actual problem.

Biological stratification of mental illness. For the patients whose conditions are genuinely rooted in neurobiology, in the hardware of the brain, we need objective tools. Biomarkers, imaging, computational models. This is where precision psychiatry and computational psychiatry come in. But this layer should be the last one we reach, not the first one we assume.

My observations were not just abstract. I saw the consequences of failing to stratify play out in real time. A patient was having an episode, and the staff were preparing to call a code grey. I decided to try to de-escalate first. I asked the patient why they thought they were having an episode. They told me it always happens before their menstrual cycle.

I told the charge nurse. The nurse said that was not correct.

But I knew it was a real phenomenon. Not because I am a clinician, but because I had read the DSM-5 from cover to cover, and Premenstrual Dysphoric Disorder is in it. It is also a recognized category in the ICD-11. There is an entire organization, the International Association for Premenstrual Disorders, dedicated to it. They even have a free self-screening tool. The patient was telling us exactly what was wrong, and the system dismissed it.

I could not push back. It was outside my scope of practice. But that moment crystallized the problem. The patient had given us the answer. We did not listen because the answer did not fit the workflow.
